nativeRange.habitat: Generally found on clear-water outer reef slopes and offshore reefs, typically at depths of about 15–200 m though the species' overall depth range extends from roughly 3 m to over 250 m; reported to favour islands and offshore reefs over continental shelves and is absent from the Persian Gulf and Hawaii.

diet.notes: Generally best fed a varied carnivore diet of meaty marine foods including krill, raw table shrimp, squid, clam, mussel, and chopped marine fish; mysis and other frozen offerings have been reported to be readily accepted by acclimated specimens. Live feeder fish are best avoided as a primary food due to nutritional inadequacy and disease-transmission risk. Has been reported to feed once or twice daily on substantial meals appropriate to its large mouth and stomach capacity.

goodWith: large predatory fish of comparable size

summary: A large Indo-Pacific predatory grouper with a distinctive lyre-shaped caudal fin and yellow fin margins; generally considered an advanced-care, ambush predator that requires a very large display and tankmates of comparable size and disposition.
careNotes:
The Red Louti Grouper (Variola louti), also widely sold as the Lyretail Grouper, Yellow-edged Lyretail, Skunk Grouper or Coronation Trout, is one of the larger species in the family Epinephelidae and one of the most demanding groupers commonly offered to home aquarists. Wild specimens have been documented to reach about 83 cm total length (FishBase), with common adult size in the 70–75 cm range; juveniles imported at 2–4 inches grow steadily and can outgrow under-sized displays within a few years.

Tank-size recommendations vary widely across hobby sources, roughly a 2x spread: LiveAquaria specifies 300 gal (~1136 L), Reeflings ~500 L (~132 gal), BlueZoo Aquatics 200 gal (~757 L), and FreshMarine 100–250 gal depending on grow-out stage. Given the species' adult size, messy carnivorous diet, need for ambush hiding spots plus generous open swimming space, and tendency to outgrow small displays, the figure used here (1000 L, ~265 gal) errs on the cautious-larger side per the predator-bias rule. Aquarists who acquire small juveniles often grow them out in smaller systems and upgrade as the fish matures.

Temperature recommendations also vary: LiveAquaria 22.2–25.6°C (72–78°F), Reeflings 22–28°C (72–82°F), and FishBase wild-occurrence data 20.1–28.9°C with a mean of about 27.2°C. The 22–27°C range used here averages across hobby and wild values. Salinity, pH, and other chemistry values are essentially consistent across sources at standard tropical reef parameters.

This species is documented as a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, consistent with the broader grouper family. Captive breeding has been reported only rarely and is not considered established hobby practice. Wild-caught juveniles are typical in the trade and display a strikingly different colour pattern from adults — orange to reddish-brown with prominent white-edged spots — which has historically caused juveniles to be mistaken for separate species.

Reef compatibility is generally listed as 'with caution': the fish poses essentially no threat to corals but has been reported to consume any fish or motile invertebrate small enough to fit in its mouth, including ornamental shrimp, small fish, and other crustaceans. It is not known to possess venomous spines, is not strictly nocturnal, and is not considered a significant jumping/escape risk for a normally enclosed marine system. The species has been reported to be relatively hardy and disease-resistant once acclimated, though it tends to be sensitive to water-quality swings given the heavy bioload its diet produces.

Cross-source care-level rating is mixed — LiveAquaria 'Moderate', Reeflings 'Moderate to difficult', BlueZoo 'Beginner'. The 'advanced' rating used here reflects the long-term housing demands (1000+ L), husbandry investment, and risk of predation on tankmates rather than any particular fragility of the fish itself.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Variola louti has been reported only very rarely in hobby literature and is not considered an established practice. As a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite, individuals begin life as female and the dominant fish in a group can transition to male upon reaching sufficient size; wild maturity is reported at around 41–48 cm. Wild reproduction is reported as pelagic spawning over reef habitat.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary product page used for hobby care values: 300 gal min, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, 30 in adult size, Moderate care, Aggressive temperament, Reef Safe with Caution, Carnivore.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Originally assigned secondary; HTTP 403 on access. Cross-verification done via the remaining slice secondaries instead.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Epinephelidae, Perciformes), maximum size (83 cm TL, common 75 cm), lifespan (7–15 yr), depth range (3–300 m, typically below 15 m), wild temperature (20.1–28.9°C, mean 27.2°C), Indo-Pacific distribution and absence from the Persian Gulf and Hawaii, and trophic level (~4.3, fish + crustacean predator).

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked aquarium care values: 500 L minimum tank, 22–28°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.026, ~70 cm adult, Moderate to difficult care, semi-aggressive/territorial, carnivore, confirmed protogynous hermaphroditism, captive breeding rare. Tank size notably smaller than LiveAquaria; the value used (1000 L) was rounded toward the cautious-larger side.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (32 in / ~81 cm), 200 gal minimum tank, temperament (aggressive ambush predator), reef-safe with caution, carnivore diet (krill/shrimp/squid/clam/mussel). BlueZoo's 'Beginner' care-level rating is an outlier vs LiveAquaria and Reeflings; not used.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been meaningfully established in the hobby. In the wild, as a protogynous hermaphrodite, dominant females transition to male upon reaching sufficient size (maturity ~41–48 cm).
